test.name: Pre-1940 census: age is indeterminate but father's birthplace is forced to secondary
test.description: On the 1900 census (src_005) the enumerator did not record who answered, so most facts are indeterminate — a_014 (Patrick's age) is correctly indeterminate. But a_015 (Patrick's FATHER's birthplace, Ireland) is stored as indeterminate too, which is wrong: no member of Patrick's household could have had first-hand knowledge of where Patrick's father was born, so the fact is SECONDARY no matter who the unknown respondent was. The skill should keep a_014 indeterminate and upgrade a_015 to secondary, explaining the special-case reasoning.

input.user_message: Check the information-quality classifications on the 1900 census assertions a_014 and a_015.

judge_context: Should KEEP a_014 (Patrick's age) as indeterminate — the 1900 census records no respondent, so who provided the age is unknown and we cannot tell if they had first-hand knowledge

Should UPGRADE a_015 (father's birthplace: Ireland) from indeterminate to SECONDARY — no one in Patrick's household witnessed his father's birth, so the fact is secondary regardless of which unknown household member answered; this is the pre-1940 census special case

Should explain WHY the two assertions from the same census get different information qualities — the special rule applies only to facts (like a grandparent-generation birthplace) that no household member could have witnessed, not to the household member's own age
